args:
{{retryContext}}

Implement ticket {{ticketId}}. Title: {{title}}. Feature reference: {{epicRef}}. Repo root: {{repoRoot}}. Run id: {{runId}}.
Ticket JSON: {{ticketFilePath}} — this file contains the full ticket data (notes, design specs, dependencies) as a live JSON document. Read it for implementation context.

SCOPE RULES (ENFORCED — violations will be rejected):
- ONLY create or modify files needed for {{ticketId}}
- Do NOT touch files from other tickets — ANY changes to files outside your ticket's scope will be REJECTED
- Do NOT fix lint errors, type errors, or test failures in files you did not create or modify
- Do not edit .ombutocode/planning/backlog.yml or .ombutocode/data/ombutocode.db
- Do NOT run git commit — leave your changes uncommitted. The scheduler handles commits automatically after your run.
- Identify your files with: git diff --name-only main...HEAD

RETRY RULES (if PREVIOUS FAILURE CONTEXT exists above):
- Your FIRST priority is fixing the SPECIFIC failing criteria listed in the retry context
- Do NOT get distracted by pre-existing lint errors or other noise — fix the failing criteria FIRST
- If the code appears complete (files exist, tests pass), the failure is likely a SUBTLE issue — read each failing criterion word-by-word and compare against the EXACT code. Passing tests do NOT prove criteria are met.
- After fixing, you MUST verify each previously-failing criterion now passes

Read the epic specification at {{epicRef}} and implement the changes described for {{ticketId}}.

Acceptance criteria:
{{acceptanceCriteria}}

Do NOT run project-wide lint or full-suite tests — the test phase handles those. You DO run your own targeted unit tests as part of the TDD cycle below.

TEST-DRIVEN DEVELOPMENT (MANDATORY — applies BEFORE you write any implementation code):
1. Detect the project test framework by reading manifest files (package.json, *.csproj, Cargo.toml, go.mod, pom.xml, requirements.txt, build.gradle, Gemfile, etc.) and the existing test directory layout. If the project has no existing test setup, scaffold one at the conventional location for the chosen language (e.g. `tests/`, `__tests__/`, `*.Tests/`).
2. Write unit tests that cover this ticket's acceptance criteria — one test per criterion where practical. Follow the project's existing test conventions (file naming, directory layout, assertion style). Test the public contract the criteria describe, not the implementation details.
3. RUN the tests now. They MUST FAIL — they are testing code you have not written yet. If any pass without an implementation, those tests are not testing the right thing — rewrite them.
4. ONLY THEN implement the production code to make the tests pass. You do NOT need to re-run the tests after implementing — the test phase that runs immediately after this one re-runs them and is the authoritative pass/fail gate.
5. In your final output, emit ONE of these markers on its own line:
   - `TESTS_ADDED: path/to/test1, path/to/test2` (relative paths, comma-separated)
   - `TESTS_SKIPPED: <specific reason>` — only for purely-UI / docs / config / data tickets where unit tests are not practical. Be specific; the test phase will reject vague rationales.
6. Also emit on its own line:
   - `TEST_COMMAND: <exact command>` — the command the test phase should run to execute your tests, scoped to just the tests you added (e.g. `dotnet test --filter FullyQualifiedName~UserAuthTests`, `npx jest path/to/foo.test.ts`, `cargo test --test foo`, `go test ./pkg/foo`, `pytest path/to/test_foo.py`).
   - Or `TEST_COMMAND: none` if TESTS_SKIPPED above.

Provide a concise summary of files changed.

RELEASE LONG-LIVED PROCESSES BEFORE EXIT (MANDATORY):
Any persistent helper you spawned during this run — a build daemon, language server, file watcher, REPL, dev server — must be shut down before you finish. These processes keep file handles open in the worktree after you exit, which on Windows blocks the scheduler's worktree cleanup and stalls the pipeline. The handle leak is not specific to any one language.

General rule: if it stayed running after your command returned, kill it. Examples for common stacks (non-exhaustive — apply whichever matches what you used):
  * .NET / MSBuild / Roslyn: `dotnet build-server shutdown`
  * Java / Gradle: `gradle --stop` (or `./gradlew --stop`)
  * Java / Maven: `mvn -T1 -q -DskipTests test` does not daemonise, but if you started `mvnDaemon`, stop it
  * Node / TypeScript: kill any `tsc --watch`, `tsserver`, `next dev`, `vite`, `webpack-dev-server` you spawned
  * Python: kill any `pytest --watch`, `uvicorn --reload`, `flask run` you spawned
  * Ruby: kill any `bundler/cli` daemons or `spring stop`
  * Rust / Go: nothing to do — neither tool keeps a daemon

Shutdown commands for stacks that have one are no-ops when no daemon is running, so it is safe to run them unconditionally.

kimi_test.stdin:
You are in TEST mode. Do NOT pick up tickets from the backlog or implement code. Your ONLY job is to run the unit tests the implementation phase added for this ticket, run lint/type checks on the changed files, and report PASS/FAIL.

Test ticket {{ticketId}} (title: {{title}}). Do not implement new code and do not edit backlog status fields.

{{retryContext}}

MANDATORY OUTPUT FORMAT — YOUR RESPONSE WILL BE MACHINE-PARSED:
Your FIRST lines of output MUST use this exact structured format. If this format is missing, the test is automatically marked FAILED.

TEST_RESULT: PASS or FAIL
UNIT_TESTS: PASS or FAIL | <details>
LINT_CHECK: PASS or FAIL | <details>
TYPE_CHECK: PASS or FAIL | <details>
FAILURE_DETAILS: <summary of what failed>

Format rules:
1. TEST_RESULT line MUST appear FIRST — use EXACTLY 'PASS' or 'FAIL'
2. All sub-checks must report PASS or FAIL with details after the pipe
3. Without 'TEST_RESULT: PASS', the tooling marks the test as FAILED
4. Output the structured format FIRST, then any additional explanation after

TESTING STEPS:

Step 0: CONSULT THE PROJECT TEST STRATEGY (if present)
- Look for `docs/Test Strategy/test-strategy.md`. If it exists, READ IT FIRST. It is the authoritative playbook for this project, written when the stack was bootstrapped. Use its commands for unit tests, lint, type-check, full-suite runs, and conventions. Treat it as more authoritative than the generic per-language guidance further down.
- If `test-strategy.md` is missing, fall back to the generic Steps 1-5 below — those handle projects that have not yet run the Initiate Stack flow.

Step 1: IDENTIFY THIS TICKET'S CHANGED FILES
- Run: git diff --name-only main...HEAD
- Split into two buckets:
  * TEST FILES — anything in a test directory (`test/`, `tests/`, `__tests__/`, `spec/`) or matching common naming patterns (`*Test*.{cs,java,kt,scala}`, `*_test.{go,py,rs}`, `*.test.{js,jsx,ts,tsx,mjs}`, `*.spec.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}`, `*_spec.rb`, `test_*.py`).
  * IMPLEMENTATION FILES — everything else (the production code this ticket added or changed).
- Only failures in files belonging to one of these two buckets count against this ticket. Pre-existing failures elsewhere are NOT this ticket's problem.

Step 2: HANDLE THE NO-TESTS CASE
- If Step 1 produced ZERO test files, look at the implementation phase output (agent stdout in the run log, or the ticket notes) for a `TESTS_SKIPPED: <reason>` marker.
  * If a `TESTS_SKIPPED:` marker is present with a specific reason, report UNIT_TESTS: PASS | tests skipped per impl-phase rationale: <quote the reason> and continue to Step 4.
  * If no skip marker is present, the impl phase violated the TDD requirement. Report UNIT_TESTS: FAIL | no tests added and no TESTS_SKIPPED rationale found — TDD requirement violated and proceed to Step 5 with overall FAIL.

Step 3: RUN THE TESTS
- Determine the project test framework from the manifest files (package.json scripts/devDependencies, *.csproj, Cargo.toml, go.mod, pom.xml/build.gradle, requirements.txt/pyproject.toml, Gemfile, etc.).
- Run ONLY the tests in the test files identified in Step 1 (not the whole suite — that may contain pre-existing failures unrelated to this ticket). Use the framework's mechanism for running specific files:
  * .NET: `dotnet test --filter "FullyQualifiedName~<class-name>"` or `dotnet test <path/to/test-project>`
  * Node / TypeScript: `npx jest <path>`, `npx vitest run <path>`, `node --test <path>`
  * Rust: `cargo test <test_name>` or `cargo test --test <file_stem>`
  * Go: `go test ./<package-path>`
  * Python: `pytest <path>` or `python -m unittest <module>`
  * Java: `mvn test -Dtest=<ClassName>` or `gradle test --tests <ClassName>`
  * Ruby: `rspec <path>` or `ruby -Itest <path>`
- If the impl agent emitted a `TEST_COMMAND:` marker in its output, prefer that exact command — it already knows which tests it wrote and how to run them.
- Capture full output with pass/fail counts. Report UNIT_TESTS: PASS or FAIL.

Step 4: LINT AND TYPE CHECK (THIS TICKET'S OWN FILES ONLY)
- Identify the project's lint and type-check tooling from the same manifest files.
- Run ONLY on files identified in Step 1 (both test and implementation files). Common patterns:
  * TypeScript: `npx eslint <files>` and `npx tsc --noEmit`
  * Rust: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
  * .NET: `dotnet build` (compile errors will surface here); `dotnet format --verify-no-changes <files>` for style
  * Go: `go vet ./...` and `gofmt -l <files>`
  * Python: `ruff <files>` and `mypy <files>` (or whichever the project uses)
  * Java: `mvn compile` and the project's configured linter (Checkstyle, SpotBugs)
- PRE-EXISTING FAILURES IN OTHER FILES DO NOT COUNT. If a lint/type error appears in a file NOT in Step 1's lists, it is not this ticket's responsibility.
- If the project has no configured lint tool, report LINT_CHECK: PASS | no linter configured. Same for TYPE_CHECK if there is no static type checker.
- Report LINT_CHECK: PASS or FAIL and TYPE_CHECK: PASS or FAIL.

Step 5: DETERMINE OVERALL RESULT
- If ALL sub-checks pass: TEST_RESULT: PASS
- If ANY sub-check fails: TEST_RESULT: FAIL with FAILURE_DETAILS explaining what failed and where

IMPORTANT: Do NOT verify acceptance criteria or epic specifications. That is the eval phase, which runs after you.

REMINDER: Start your response with the structured format (TEST_RESULT, UNIT_TESTS, LINT_CHECK, TYPE_CHECK).

RELEASE LONG-LIVED PROCESSES BEFORE EXIT (MANDATORY):
Any persistent helper you spawned during this run — a build daemon, language server, file watcher, REPL, dev server — must be shut down before you finish. These processes keep file handles open in the worktree after you exit, which on Windows blocks the scheduler's worktree cleanup and stalls the pipeline. The handle leak is not specific to any one language.

General rule: if it stayed running after your command returned, kill it. Examples for common stacks (non-exhaustive — apply whichever matches what you used):
  * .NET / MSBuild / Roslyn: `dotnet build-server shutdown`
  * Java / Gradle: `gradle --stop` (or `./gradlew --stop`)
  * Java / Maven: `mvn -T1 -q -DskipTests test` does not daemonise, but if you started `mvnDaemon`, stop it
  * Node / TypeScript: kill any `tsc --watch`, `tsserver`, `next dev`, `vite`, `webpack-dev-server` you spawned
  * Python: kill any `pytest --watch`, `uvicorn --reload`, `flask run` you spawned
  * Ruby: kill any `bundler/cli` daemons or `spring stop`
  * Rust / Go: nothing to do — neither tool keeps a daemon

Shutdown commands for stacks that have one are no-ops when no daemon is running, so it is safe to run them unconditionally.

kimi_eval.stdin:
You are in EVAL mode. Do NOT pick up tickets from the backlog or implement code. Your ONLY job is to evaluate the ticket below.

Evaluate ticket {{ticketId}} (title: {{title}}) against epic reference {{epicRef}}. Do not implement new code and do not edit backlog status fields.

IMPORTANT: Unit tests, lint, and type-checking have already passed in the test phase. Do NOT re-run them. Focus ONLY on acceptance criteria and epic spec verification.

MANDATORY OUTPUT FORMAT — YOUR RESPONSE WILL BE MACHINE-PARSED:
Your FIRST lines of output MUST use this exact structured format. If this format is missing, the evaluation is automatically marked FAILED regardless of your actual findings.

EVALUATION_RESULT: PASS or FAIL
ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA_CHECKS:
- PASS: <criterion text> | evidence: <specific file/line evidence>
- FAIL: <criterion text> | failure_reason: <detailed reason> | suggestion: <actionable fix>
EPIC_REFERENCE_CHECK: PASS or FAIL | evidence: <verification against epic spec>
SUMMARY: <one-line verdict>

Format rules:
1. EVALUATION_RESULT line MUST appear FIRST — use EXACTLY 'PASS' or 'FAIL'
2. Each criterion MUST be on a bullet line starting with '- PASS:' or '- FAIL:'
3. For FAIL criteria, you MUST include failure_reason and suggestion fields
4. Without 'EVALUATION_RESULT: PASS', the tooling marks evaluation as FAILED
5. Output the structured format FIRST, then any additional explanation after

EVALUATION STEPS:

Step 1: IDENTIFY TICKET FILES
- Run: git diff --name-only main...HEAD
- These are the ticket's own files. Verify all expected files from ticket scope exist.

Step 2: ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA VERIFICATION
Do NOT look up criteria from backlog.yml — use ONLY these:
{{acceptanceCriteria}}
Check each criterion with specific evidence (file paths, line numbers). If ANY fails: EVALUATION_RESULT MUST be FAIL.

Step 3: EPIC SPECIFICATION VERIFICATION
Verify implementation matches epic spec at {{epicRef}}. If FAIL: EVALUATION_RESULT MUST be FAIL.

REMINDER: Start your response with the structured format (EVALUATION_RESULT, ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA_CHECKS, etc.). Narrative-only output CANNOT be parsed and will be marked FAILED by the tooling.

RELEASE LONG-LIVED PROCESSES BEFORE EXIT (MANDATORY):
Any persistent helper you spawned during this run — a build daemon, language server, file watcher, REPL, dev server — must be shut down before you finish. These processes keep file handles open in the worktree after you exit, which on Windows blocks the scheduler's worktree cleanup and stalls the pipeline. The handle leak is not specific to any one language.

General rule: if it stayed running after your command returned, kill it. Examples for common stacks (non-exhaustive — apply whichever matches what you used):

  * Node / TypeScript: kill any `tsc --watch`, `tsserver`, `next dev`, `vite`, `webpack-dev-server` you spawned
  * Python: kill any `pytest --watch`, `uvicorn --reload`, `flask run` you spawned
  * Ruby: kill any `bundler/cli` daemons or `spring stop`
  * Rust / Go: nothing to do — neither tool keeps a daemon

Shutdown commands for stacks that have one are no-ops when no daemon is running, so it is safe to run them unconditionally.

{{retryContext}}

Implement ticket {{ticketId}}. Title: {{title}}. Feature reference: {{epicRef}}. Repo root: {{workingDirectory}}. Run id: {{runId}}.

SCOPE RULES (ENFORCED — violations will be rejected):
- ONLY create or modify files needed for {{ticketId}}

- Do NOT fix lint errors, type errors, or test failures in files you did not create or modify
- Do not edit .ombutocode/planning/backlog.yml or .ombutocode/data/ombutocode.db
- Do NOT run git commit — leave your changes uncommitted. The scheduler handles commits automatically after your run.
- Identify your files with: git diff --name-only main...HEAD

RETRY RULES (if PREVIOUS FAILURE CONTEXT exists above):
- Your FIRST priority is fixing the SPECIFIC failing criteria listed in the retry context
- Do NOT get distracted by pre-existing lint errors or other noise — fix the failing criteria FIRST
- If the code appears complete (files exist, tests pass), the failure is likely a SUBTLE issue — read each failing criterion word-by-word and compare against the EXACT code. Passing tests do NOT prove criteria are met.
- After fixing, you MUST verify each previously-failing criterion now passes

Read the epic specification at {{epicRef}} and implement the changes described for {{ticketId}}.

Acceptance criteria:
{{acceptanceCriteria}}

Do NOT run project-wide lint or full-suite tests — the test phase handles those. You DO run your own targeted unit tests as part of the TDD cycle below.

TEST-DRIVEN DEVELOPMENT (MANDATORY — applies BEFORE you write any implementation code):
1. Detect the project test framework by reading manifest files (package.json, *.csproj, Cargo.toml, go.mod, pom.xml, requirements.txt, build.gradle, Gemfile, etc.) and the existing test directory layout. If the project has no existing test setup, scaffold one at the conventional location for the chosen language (e.g. `tests/`, `__tests__/`, `*.Tests/`).
2. Write unit tests that cover this ticket's acceptance criteria — one test per criterion where practical. Follow the project's existing test conventions (file naming, directory layout, assertion style). Test the public contract the criteria describe, not the implementation details.
3. RUN the tests now. They MUST FAIL — they are testing code you have not written yet. If any pass without an implementation, those tests are not testing the right thing — rewrite them.
4. ONLY THEN implement the production code to make the tests pass. You do NOT need to re-run the tests after implementing — the test phase that runs immediately after this one re-runs them and is the authoritative pass/fail gate.
5. In your final output, emit ONE of these markers on its own line:
   - `TESTS_ADDED: path/to/test1, path/to/test2` (relative paths, comma-separated)
   - `TESTS_SKIPPED: <specific reason>` — only for purely-UI / docs / config / data tickets where unit tests are not practical. Be specific; the test phase will reject vague rationales.
6. Also emit on its own line:
   - `TEST_COMMAND: <exact command>` — the command the test phase should run to execute your tests, scoped to just the tests you added (e.g. `dotnet test --filter FullyQualifiedName~UserAuthTests`, `npx jest path/to/foo.test.ts`, `cargo test --test foo`, `go test ./pkg/foo`, `pytest path/to/test_foo.py`).

claude_test.stdin:
You are in TEST mode. Do NOT pick up tickets from the backlog or implement code. Your ONLY job is to run the unit tests the implementation phase added for this ticket, run lint/type checks on the changed files, and report PASS/FAIL.

Test ticket {{ticketId}} (title: {{title}}). Do not implement new code and do not edit backlog status fields.

{{retryContext}}

MANDATORY OUTPUT FORMAT — YOUR RESPONSE WILL BE MACHINE-PARSED:
Your FIRST lines of output MUST use this exact structured format. If this format is missing, the test is automatically marked FAILED.

TEST_RESULT: PASS or FAIL
UNIT_TESTS: PASS or FAIL | <details>
LINT_CHECK: PASS or FAIL | <details>
TYPE_CHECK: PASS or FAIL | <details>
FAILURE_DETAILS: <summary of what failed>

Format rules:
1. TEST_RESULT line MUST appear FIRST — use EXACTLY 'PASS' or 'FAIL'
2. All sub-checks must report PASS or FAIL with details after the pipe
3. Without 'TEST_RESULT: PASS', the tooling marks the test as FAILED
4. Output the structured format FIRST, then any additional explanation after

TESTING STEPS:

Step 0: CONSULT THE PROJECT TEST STRATEGY (if present)
- Look for `docs/Test Strategy/test-strategy.md`. If it exists, READ IT FIRST. It is the authoritative playbook for this project, written when the stack was bootstrapped. Use its commands for unit tests, lint, type-check, full-suite runs, and conventions. Treat it as more authoritative than the generic per-language guidance further down.
- If `test-strategy.md` is missing, fall back to the generic Steps 1-5 below — those handle projects that have not yet run the Initiate Stack flow.

Step 1: IDENTIFY THIS TICKET'S CHANGED FILES
- Run: git diff --name-only main...HEAD
- Split into two buckets:
  * TEST FILES — anything in a test directory (`test/`, `tests/`, `__tests__/`, `spec/`) or matching common naming patterns (`*Test*.{cs,java,kt,scala}`, `*_test.{go,py,rs}`, `*.test.{js,jsx,ts,tsx,mjs}`, `*.spec.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}`, `*_spec.rb`, `test_*.py`).
  * IMPLEMENTATION FILES — everything else (the production code this ticket added or changed).
- Only failures in files belonging to one of these two buckets count against this ticket. Pre-existing failures elsewhere are NOT this ticket's problem.

Step 2: HANDLE THE NO-TESTS CASE
- If Step 1 produced ZERO test files, look at the implementation phase output (agent stdout in the run log, or the ticket notes) for a `TESTS_SKIPPED: <reason>` marker.
  * If a `TESTS_SKIPPED:` marker is present with a specific reason, report UNIT_TESTS: PASS | tests skipped per impl-phase rationale: <quote the reason> and continue to Step 4.
  * If no skip marker is present, the impl phase violated the TDD requirement. Report UNIT_TESTS: FAIL | no tests added and no TESTS_SKIPPED rationale found — TDD requirement violated and proceed to Step 5 with overall FAIL.

Step 3: RUN THE TESTS
- Determine the project test framework from the manifest files (package.json scripts/devDependencies, *.csproj, Cargo.toml, go.mod, pom.xml/build.gradle, requirements.txt/pyproject.toml, Gemfile, etc.).
- Run ONLY the tests in the test files identified in Step 1 (not the whole suite — that may contain pre-existing failures unrelated to this ticket). Use the framework's mechanism for running specific files:
  * .NET: `dotnet test --filter "FullyQualifiedName~<class-name>"` or `dotnet test <path/to/test-project>`
  * Node / TypeScript: `npx jest <path>`, `npx vitest run <path>`, `node --test <path>`
  * Rust: `cargo test <test_name>` or `cargo test --test <file_stem>`
  * Go: `go test ./<package-path>`
  * Python: `pytest <path>` or `python -m unittest <module>`
  * Java: `mvn test -Dtest=<ClassName>` or `gradle test --tests <ClassName>`
  * Ruby: `rspec <path>` or `ruby -Itest <path>`
- If the impl agent emitted a `TEST_COMMAND:` marker in its output, prefer that exact command — it already knows which tests it wrote and how to run them.
- Capture full output with pass/fail counts. Report UNIT_TESTS: PASS or FAIL.

Step 4: LINT AND TYPE CHECK (THIS TICKET'S OWN FILES ONLY)
- Identify the project's lint and type-check tooling from the same manifest files.
- Run ONLY on files identified in Step 1 (both test and implementation files). Common patterns:
  * TypeScript: `npx eslint <files>` and `npx tsc --noEmit`
  * Rust: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
  * .NET: `dotnet build` (compile errors will surface here); `dotnet format --verify-no-changes <files>` for style
  * Go: `go vet ./...` and `gofmt -l <files>`
  * Python: `ruff <files>` and `mypy <files>` (or whichever the project uses)
  * Java: `mvn compile` and the project's configured linter (Checkstyle, SpotBugs)
- PRE-EXISTING FAILURES IN OTHER FILES DO NOT COUNT. If a lint/type error appears in a file NOT in Step 1's lists, it is not this ticket's responsibility.
- If the project has no configured lint tool, report LINT_CHECK: PASS | no linter configured. Same for TYPE_CHECK if there is no static type checker.
- Report LINT_CHECK: PASS or FAIL and TYPE_CHECK: PASS or FAIL.

Step 5: DETERMINE OVERALL RESULT
- If ALL sub-checks pass: TEST_RESULT: PASS
- If ANY sub-check fails: TEST_RESULT: FAIL with FAILURE_DETAILS explaining what failed and where

IMPORTANT: Do NOT verify acceptance criteria or epic specifications. That is the eval phase, which runs after you.

REMINDER: Start your response with the structured format (TEST_RESULT, UNIT_TESTS, LINT_CHECK, TYPE_CHECK).

RELEASE LONG-LIVED PROCESSES BEFORE EXIT (MANDATORY):
Any persistent helper you spawned during this run — a build daemon, language server, file watcher, REPL, dev server — must be shut down before you finish. These processes keep file handles open in the worktree after you exit, which on Windows blocks the scheduler's worktree cleanup and stalls the pipeline. The handle leak is not specific to any one language.

General rule: if it stayed running after your command returned, kill it. Examples for common stacks (non-exhaustive — apply whichever matches what you used):
  * .NET / MSBuild / Roslyn: `dotnet build-server shutdown`
  * Java / Gradle: `gradle --stop` (or `./gradlew --stop`)
  * Java / Maven: `mvn -T1 -q -DskipTests test` does not daemonise, but if you started `mvnDaemon`, stop it
  * Node / TypeScript: kill any `tsc --watch`, `tsserver`, `next dev`, `vite`, `webpack-dev-server` you spawned
  * Python: kill any `pytest --watch`, `uvicorn --reload`, `flask run` you spawned
  * Ruby: kill any `bundler/cli` daemons or `spring stop`
  * Rust / Go: nothing to do — neither tool keeps a daemon

Shutdown commands for stacks that have one are no-ops when no daemon is running, so it is safe to run them unconditionally.

- FAIL: <criterion text> | failure_reason: <detailed reason> | suggestion: <actionable fix>

2. Each criterion MUST be on a bullet line starting with '- PASS:' or '- FAIL:'
3. For FAIL criteria, you MUST include failure_reason and suggestion fields
4. Without 'EVALUATION_RESULT: PASS', the tooling marks evaluation as FAILED
5. Output the structured format FIRST, then any additional explanation after

Any persistent helper you spawned during this run — a build daemon, language server, file watcher, REPL, dev server — must be shut down before you finish.   * .NET / MSBuild / Roslyn: `dotnet build-server shutdown`
  * Java / Gradle: `gradle --stop` (or `./gradlew --stop`)
  * Java / Maven: `mvn -T1 -q -DskipTests test` does not daemonise, but if you started `mvnDaemon`, stop it
  * Node / TypeScript: kill any `tsc --watch`, `tsserver`, `next dev`, `vite`, `webpack-dev-server` you spawned

args:
Merge-resolve ticket {{ticketId}} (title: {{title}}). Repo root: {{repoRoot}}. Run id: {{runId}}.

The ticket branch ticket/{{ticketId}} has diverged from main. Your job is to rebase the branch onto main, resolving any conflicts if they arise. If the rebase succeeds with NO conflicts, that is a successful outcome.

AUTONOMOUS TASK: This is a fully autonomous operation. NEVER ask clarifying questions. NEVER request user input. Follow the steps below and output your result.

STEP-BY-STEP PROCEDURE (MANDATORY):

Step 1: ASSESS THE SITUATION
- Run: git log --oneline main..HEAD
- Run: git diff main...HEAD --stat
- Run: git log --oneline HEAD..main (to see what main has that the branch doesn't)
- Identify which files will likely conflict

CRITICAL — CHECK FOR INHERITED COMMITS:
Examine the output of `git log --oneline main..HEAD`. If ANY commits reference a different ticket ID (e.g., OMB-XXX where XXX is not {{ticketId}}), the branch carries INHERITED commits from a parent ticket. This happens when the branch was created from another ticket branch instead of from main.
- Count how many commits belong to {{ticketId}} vs other tickets
- Note the hash of the PARENT of the first commit that belongs to {{ticketId}}
- You MUST use the --onto rebase strategy in Step 2 if inherited commits exist

Step 2: REBASE ONTO MAIN

Option A — No inherited commits (ALL commits in `git log main..HEAD` belong to {{ticketId}}):
- Run: git rebase main
- If conflicts occur, proceed to Step 3
- If rebase completes with NO conflicts, this is a success — skip directly to Step 4

Option B — Inherited commits detected (commits from other tickets exist):
Plain `git rebase main` will replay inherited commits and cause spurious conflicts with code already on main. Instead:
- Identify <parent-hash>: the commit hash of the parent of this ticket's first own commit
  Example: if log shows:
    abc1234 {{ticketId}}: Implementation changes     <- this ticket
    def5678 OMB-014: Add resolveCoreBinary()          <- inherited
    ghi9012 OMB-014: Implementation changes           <- inherited
  Then <parent-hash> is def5678
- Run: git rebase --onto main <parent-hash> ticket/{{ticketId}}
  This replays ONLY commits belonging to {{ticketId}}, skipping inherited ones
- If conflicts occur, proceed to Step 3
- If rebase completes with NO conflicts, this is a success — skip directly to Step 4

Step 3: RESOLVE EACH CONFLICT
For each conflicting file:
- Read the file to understand the conflict markers (<<<<<<< HEAD, =======, >>>>>>> ...)
- During rebase, HEAD is main's version and the incoming side is the ticket branch's version
- Decide the correct resolution based on conflict type:

  MODIFY/MODIFY CONFLICTS (both sides changed the same file):
  - MERGE BOTH SIDES: keep changes from both main and the ticket branch
  - For Cargo.toml / package.json: merge dependency lists, keep both additions
  - For mod.rs / lib.rs: combine module declarations from both sides
  - For TypeScript/Rust source files: integrate imports, types, and logic from both sides
  - For config/data files (JSON, YAML, TOML): merge both additions

  ADD/ADD CONFLICTS (both sides created the same new file):
  - Read BOTH versions carefully to understand their intent
  - If one is a superset of the other, keep the more complete version
  - If both add unique content, combine them into a single coherent file
  - Pay special attention to imports, exports, and module registrations

- Write the resolved file (remove ALL conflict markers: <<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>)
- Verify no conflict markers remain: grep -rn '<<<<<<< ' <file> (must return nothing)
- Run: git add <resolved_file>
- Run: git -c core.editor=true rebase --continue
- Repeat for any subsequent conflicts in the rebase

Step 4: VERIFY THE RESULT
- Run: git log --oneline main..HEAD (confirm clean history — should only contain {{ticketId}} commits)
- Run: git diff main...HEAD --stat (confirm expected file changes)
- Run: grep -rn '<<<<<<< ' . --include='*.rs' --include='*.ts' --include='*.js' --include='*.json' --include='*.toml' (confirm no leftover conflict markers)
- Ensure all ticket files still exist and contain the expected content
NOTE: Testing will be handled by the test phase after merge. Only verify no conflict markers remain and files are intact.

FALLBACK — If rebase produces too many conflicts or cascading failures:
- Run: git rebase --abort
- Try: git merge main (merge commit instead of rebase)
- Resolve conflicts using the same rules from Step 3
- If merge also fails, report FAIL with details

Step 5: OUTPUT RESULT
After completing all steps above, output the result in this exact format:

MERGE_RESOLVE_RESULT: SUCCESS
or
MERGE_RESOLVE_RESULT: FAIL
FAILURE_REASON: <detailed explanation>

CRITICAL: The line 'MERGE_RESOLVE_RESULT: SUCCESS' or 'MERGE_RESOLVE_RESULT: FAIL' MUST appear in your output. Without it, the system treats the run as a failure regardless of actual outcome.

Rules:
- Do NOT modify any ticket implementation code beyond what is needed to resolve conflicts
- Do NOT edit .ombutocode/planning/backlog.yml or the ombutocode.db database
- Do NOT fix pre-existing test/lint failures — only ensure no NEW failures are introduced
- If the rebase/merge cannot be completed, abort and report FAIL

{{retryContext}}

Step 5: OUTPUT RESULT
After completing all steps above, output the result in this exact format:

MERGE_RESOLVE_RESULT: SUCCESS
or
MERGE_RESOLVE_RESULT: FAIL
FAILURE_REASON: <detailed explanation>

CRITICAL: The line 'MERGE_RESOLVE_RESULT: SUCCESS' or 'MERGE_RESOLVE_RESULT: FAIL' MUST appear in your output. Without it, the system treats the run as a failure regardless of actual outcome.

- If rebase completes with NO conflicts, this is a success — skip directly to Step 4

Option B — Inherited commits detected (commits from other tickets exist):
Plain `git rebase main` will replay inherited commits and cause spurious conflicts with code already on main. Instead:
- Identify <parent-hash>: the commit hash of the parent of this ticket's first own commit
  Example: if log shows:
    abc1234 {{ticketId}}: Implementation changes     <- this ticket
    def5678 OMB-014: Add resolveCoreBinary()          <- inherited
    ghi9012 OMB-014: Implementation changes           <- inherited
  Then <parent-hash> is def5678
- Run: git rebase --onto main <parent-hash> ticket/{{ticketId}}
  This replays ONLY commits belonging to {{ticketId}}, skipping inherited ones
- If conflicts occur, proceed to Step 3
- If rebase completes with NO conflicts, this is a success — skip directly to Step 4

Step 3: RESOLVE EACH CONFLICT
For each conflicting file:
- Read the file to understand the conflict markers (<<<<<<< HEAD, =======, >>>>>>> ...)
- During rebase, HEAD is main's version and the incoming side is the ticket branch's version
- Decide the correct resolution based on conflict type:

  MODIFY/MODIFY CONFLICTS (both sides changed the same file):
  - MERGE BOTH SIDES: keep changes from both main and the ticket branch
  - For Cargo.toml / package.json: merge dependency lists, keep both additions
  - For mod.rs / lib.rs: combine module declarations from both sides
  - For TypeScript/Rust source files: integrate imports, types, and logic from both sides
  - For config/data files (JSON, YAML, TOML): merge both additions

  ADD/ADD CONFLICTS (both sides created the same new file):
  - Read BOTH versions carefully to understand their intent
  - If one is a superset of the other, keep the more complete version
  - If both add unique content, combine them into a single coherent file
  - Pay special attention to imports, exports, and module registrations

- Write the resolved file (remove ALL conflict markers: <<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>)
- Verify no conflict markers remain: grep -rn '<<<<<<< ' <file> (must return nothing)
- Run: git add <resolved_file>
- Run: git -c core.editor=true rebase --continue
- Repeat for any subsequent conflicts in the rebase

Step 4: VERIFY THE RESULT
- Run: git log --oneline main..HEAD (confirm clean history — should only contain {{ticketId}} commits)
- Run: git diff main...HEAD --stat (confirm expected file changes)
- Run: grep -rn '<<<<<<< ' . --include='*.rs' --include='*.ts' --include='*.js' --include='*.json' --include='*.toml' (confirm no leftover conflict markers)
- Ensure all ticket files still exist and contain the expected content
NOTE: Testing will be handled by the test phase after merge. Only verify no conflict markers remain and files are intact.

FALLBACK — If rebase produces too many conflicts or cascading failures:
- Run: git rebase --abort
- Try: git merge main (merge commit instead of rebase)
- Resolve conflicts using the same rules from Step 3
- If merge also fails, report FAIL with details

Step 5: OUTPUT RESULT
After completing all steps above, output the result in this exact format:

MERGE_RESOLVE_RESULT: SUCCESS
or
MERGE_RESOLVE_RESULT: FAIL
FAILURE_REASON: <detailed explanation>

CRITICAL: The line 'MERGE_RESOLVE_RESULT: SUCCESS' or 'MERGE_RESOLVE_RESULT: FAIL' MUST appear in your output. Without it, the system treats the run as a failure regardless of actual outcome.

Rules:
- Do NOT modify any ticket implementation code beyond what is needed to resolve conflicts
- Do NOT edit .ombutocode/planning/backlog.yml or the ombutocode.db database
- Do NOT fix pre-existing test/lint failures — only ensure no NEW failures are introduced
- If the rebase/merge cannot be completed, abort and report FAIL

{{retryContext}}

kimi_epic_eval.stdin:
You are in EPIC EVALUATION mode. Do NOT pick up tickets from the backlog or implement code. Your ONLY job is to evaluate whether the epic described below has been fully implemented across ALL its linked tickets.

Epic: {{title}}
Epic specification: {{epicRef}}
Repo root: {{repoRoot}}

IMPORTANT: This is a epic-level evaluation, not a single ticket evaluation. You must verify that ALL ticket acceptance criteria are met AND that the epic specification requirements are satisfied holistically.

MANDATORY OUTPUT FORMAT — YOUR RESPONSE WILL BE MACHINE-PARSED:
Your FIRST lines of output MUST use this exact structured format. If this format is missing, the evaluation is automatically marked FAILED.

EPIC_EVALUATION_RESULT: PASS or FAIL
TICKETS_VERIFIED:
- PASS: <ticket_id> | <ticket_title> | all criteria marked [x]
- FAIL: <ticket_id> | <ticket_title> | failure_reason: <reason>
CRITERIA_STATUS: <total_checked>/<total_criteria> criteria marked complete
EPIC_SPEC_CHECK: PASS or FAIL | evidence: <verification summary>
SUMMARY: <one-line verdict>

Format rules:
1. EPIC_EVALUATION_RESULT line MUST appear FIRST — use EXACTLY 'PASS' or 'FAIL'
2. Each ticket MUST be listed under TICKETS_VERIFIED with PASS or FAIL
3. For FAIL tickets, include failure_reason
4. Without 'EPIC_EVALUATION_RESULT: PASS', the tooling marks evaluation as FAILED
5. Output the structured format FIRST, then any additional explanation after

EVALUATION STEPS:

Step 1: READ THE EPIC SPECIFICATION
- Read the epic spec file at {{epicRef}}
- Understand the overall epic goals and requirements

Step 2: VERIFY TICKET ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA ARE COMPLETE
Each ticket's acceptance criteria should already be marked as complete ([x]) from prior ticket-level evaluations. Criteria prefixed with [x] have passed eval; criteria prefixed with [ ] have NOT passed.

The following tickets and their acceptance criteria must ALL be verified:

{{acceptanceCriteria}}

For each ticket:
- Confirm ALL acceptance criteria are marked [x] (complete). Any criterion still marked [ ] means the ticket has not fully passed its eval and the epic CANNOT pass.
- Cross-verify against the actual codebase: check that the implementation evidence exists (file paths, exports, test coverage) for each criterion.
- A ticket PASSES only when every one of its criteria is both marked [x] AND verified in the codebase.

Step 3: VERIFY EPIC-LEVEL REQUIREMENTS
Beyond individual ticket criteria, verify:
- The epic works as a cohesive whole (not just individual pieces)
- Integration points between tickets are correct
- The epic spec's high-level requirements are met
- No gaps exist between ticket implementations
- The epic spec's own acceptance criteria section (if present) has all items satisfied

Step 4: DETERMINE OVERALL RESULT
- ALL tickets must have ALL criteria marked [x] and verified for the epic to PASS
- The epic spec check must PASS for the epic to PASS
- If ANY ticket has unchecked criteria ([ ]) or the epic spec check fails: EPIC_EVALUATION_RESULT: FAIL

REMINDER: Start your response with the structured format. Narrative-only output CANNOT be parsed and will be marked FAILED by the tooling.
